summary: McKenzie shares an amusing example of a Japanese web service that converts text messages into schoolgirl leetspeak, charging about 5 cents per message via phone billing. Despite the service being trivially simple to build—essentially a short Perl script and a translation table—it processes 20 million messages monthly. The company also charges to decrypt the gibberish back into readable Japanese, effectively monetizing both directions of the same problem.
key_points: A trivially simple hosted service (15 lines of Perl + a lookup table) generates massive revenue by processing 20 million messages monthly at 5 cents each
The service solves a real pain point: typing stylized text is annoying on Japanese cellphones, so automation has genuine value
Charging for both encryption and decryption of the same gibberish is a brilliantly absurd double-dip business model
Mobile carrier billing infrastructure enables frictionless micropayments that make the economics work
money_quotes: Sounds like a complete waste of time, right? Yeah, probably… unless you process 20 million messages a month.
For a service which is, approximately, a 15 line Perl script and a 100 line Japanese-to-gibberish table of rules.
I was particularly impressed by the for-pay option to decrypt what your friend was trying to say to you in case you were in no mood to parse it yourself.
key_insight: Enormous businesses can be built from trivially simple software when you solve a genuine friction point at scale with frictionless payments.
